titleStmt.title: Jordan – Bani Saxar – Rujm Ash Shami – Family; Wedding; Food Recipes
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Jordan Fieldwork Campaign Antonella Torzullo 2022 OEAW

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: First thing first, tell us your name
My name is ***
The complete (one)
From the Bani Ṣaxar tribe, the Xaḏ̣īr of Bani Ṣaxar
Your full name
My name is ***
*** how?
Yes
From the bani ṣaxar tribe, from the xaḏ̣īr of the Bani Ṣaxar
From the Xaḏ̣īr of the Bani Ṣaxar. How old are you?
I am 44 years old.
Where do you live?
I live in al-ma(w)gar luwā (add accent), luwā (add accent) al-Ma(w)gar ruǧm aš-šāmi, the village of Ruǧm aš-Šāmi al-Ġarbi
And have you always lived in the same place?
Yes, since I was born, and God willing I (will) die in Ruǧm aš-Šāmi
Where did you study?
I studied in the high school of ruǧm aš-šāmi, at the beginning it was mixed
from first class, second class, third, until 4th class, then no, in 4th class, no after it finished, it started (?) middle school, and then high school
and of course it was girls school... the boys alone and the girls alone / separated
Did you go to the university or not?
No, by God I did not.
I got married once I finished hight school, Abu ʕAbdallah bethroted me and I married him.
How old where you when you got married?
I was 18 years old.
Where is your husband from?
My husband is also from the Xaḏ̣īr, from my relatives (people)
Do you go to Amman? Do you go sometimes?
Yes we go sometimes, we go out buying stuff from Amman, going shopping
Yes, we don't always go to Amman
Ah, not always
Not always, I mean
When it is needed (lit. in time of need)
When it is needed (lit. in time of need) or we go there when we want to a change of scene (lit. change the air), going out, buying things
But we don't always go to Amman (richiamo del soggetto- sogg a sin con poi pronome che richiama)
Fine, let's talk about the engagement and the wedding of the Bani Ṣaxar
Of the Bani Ṣaxar or mine?
I mean, the one you want, you are a Ṣxariyya, I mean [let’s] talk about the engagement…
Fine, for the Bani Ṣaxar the first thing is…
Which are the traditions?
[for] the Bani Ṣaxar …the tradition is followed by almost all of us
It is to say most of the people are engaged with a one of their relatives, usually the girl does not go away [from the family]
and the very most part take the cousin, but if he is not available sometimes she goes away… the important thing is that we are almost all [with] a cousin
The closer with the closer and the nearer with the nearer
But thanks God I took one which was not my relative, not my proper cousin, only from the same branch of the tribe
from the same branch of the tribe, [ā] Xaḏ̣īr
From the same Xaḏ̣īr
From the same Xaḏ̣īr
[Before] the people tell him about her, his brothers, and other people
as first thing they sent a small ǧāha (group), his father, his mother,
his uncle, I mean from members of his family, they go see the girl
Ok..

They talk to her father, of course her father asks her opinion, thanks God for us the opinion is compulsory for the girl, they have to ask the girl, only few times there are few families who do not ask her opinion, but for the most part they do
yes, if the girl agrees, they make the engagement and bring a bigger group of representatives [of the groom’s family] and decide the dowry
How much is the dowery?
Usually between close relatives the dowry is not that high…
Oh so between the relatives it is not high
Oh so between the relatives it is not high, U: yeah, between relatives they are always sympathetic/they take care of each other
So the dowry is the bride’s share
Yes, the dowry is the bride’s share , I mean the dowry of my turn/ period when I got married, around the 94
It was 1500 of gold [and] 3000 mʔaǧǧal
and he gave us forniture for the house, he gave the forniture for his house, and the most part live (?) with (within) the family, at his father, and his mother and his parents
rarely there is one, who builds a house and goes to live alone (lit. went out alone)
This was before?
Yes, in my time... in the time before '94... since the 90s, they stopped, meaning now it's rare
they get married in the family unless he builds a house
he builds a house for himself
who has a house alone and lives in it alone
Which is better, living alone or with family
In the time of our ancestors, the father used to build a tent.
Ah... and he would bring (or prepare) a horse for the boy, and there was a trunk for the bride, meaning they would celebrate her, and the bride wouldn’t know the groom or see him
until the wedding night; they don’t see each other
Never? / at all?
They prepare the trunk for her, the bridal trunk
meaning they buy her a few things [to put in) it.
And that’s it; they didn’t consult them before. It was rare for them to consult them
this is the story before the 70ies.
Before the 70ies?
Yes, but thank God, Lord of the Worlds, most people, thank God, have faith and consult the girl, and they don’t give the girl away unless she agrees and is accepting of the marriage
Fine and now?
They do the wedding, during my time, before, the wedding would last 7 days, from Saturday until Friday
they pitch up tents
From Saturday until Friday, 7 days
Yes 7 days, those who do not do 7 days are rare, unless...
they dance at night, and they sing (lit. they do this dance and this song) and they perform hǧēni and poetry.
This is at the groom´s place, the wedding of 7 days
Yes, this is at the groom´s, at the groom´s family of course... the bride has a night on Wednesday or Thursday
they do the ḥinna, the ḥinna of the bride, her female relatives and friends
they come and have the party at home.
They come, make a party for her, give her money (nqūt: giving money to the bride for blessings), congratulate her (lit. bless her). In my time, this is what was done, and before that..
There was nothing
No, these things were there, of course.
But there weren´t during your time
No, there were in my time, how (comes)?
It means you did the ḥenna party?
I had a ḥenna party, like on Wednesday, and a wedding for seven days with songs and dancing
And a group of singers comes ... In my time, before, they sing the hǧēni, performed by the very same people (the same people who were there would perform the hǧēni )
and then they hold the dinner night... the night of celebration for the groom.
They prepare them dinner, to the group that goes to bring the bride. The group, of course, refers to the people who go to fetch the bride from her family's house
to the house of the groom
cars of course
celebrate the briden [conduct in solemn procession lit.]
And the next day, Friday, they perform the ǧirā́
The ǧirā́ is the wedding meal
they make a big meal, it means the father slaughters for him 10 - 11 pieces of livestock
It means they invite all their relatives
Yes, of course, they invite them and send the invitation about two weeks before the wedding or something to the relatives
The relatives invite them personally, while the people from outside send them wedding cards... invitations.
This was before?
This is what happened (lit. the story) in my time in the 90s and before the 90s
Before that no, from the time of...
Before the 70s and before that, there were no invitation cards or anything
There was nothing?
No
A: Okay, so what is there... I mean, now, your engagement, so tell us about yourself.
About my engagement?
Your engagement, did you see my father? Did you spend time with him [sit with him] before the engagement? Was there...
There was an acquaintance, of course; our relatives knew each other... we know each other... I mean, he knows me and I know hi
They came and proposed to me, he sent his uncle and father
sent his cousin (patrilinear), he becomes my sister's fiancé
and his female cousin becomes my brother's wife
Ok
They sent them and asked me (my hand), and of course, my father consulted me and they consulted me... and we agreed
the engagement lasted 14 days; there wasn't a lengthy engagement period
During my time, some people would have an engagement for a year, six months... I mean, no one would stay engaged for more than a year.
But for you and my father was quick
No, we (did it) immediately, 14 days, and we got married
We spent 14 days preparing for the wedding (Lit. 14 days of course, it was the preparation for the wedding)
He probably came to us twice... once, he brought me the dowry, the payment of the gold (?)
And once, he came to chat/spend the evening chatting. So, he only came to us twice while I was engaged
Of course, during the 14 days, I went to buy things to prepare the bride's trousseau/ attire
he prepares the household necessities
So, what do you prepare, like what I mean?
I mean clothes, a bedroom
Ok
That is it, I did not live with family, in my time
You lived with my father and his family?
Yes, I lived with his family, in their house, in a room, and the kitchen is shared, and the bathroom is shared
How many brothers did he have?
He had, Mashallah, two brothers, and his father was an old man, and his sister... his sister got married before I did, I mean
she didn't stay with them long
she didn´t stay long, I mean, 6 months we stayed/lived together
So, what kind of food is specific to the Bedouins, to the bani ṣaxar?
For the wedding or...?
A: for the wedding or in general, what was the food?
U:By God, at a wedding, manāsif. In the old days, did not we say it, they would celebrate the wedding for seven days. Sometimes they would make rušūf,
Rušūf
They would make fuwwīr
What is this fuwwīr?
To make fuwwīr, they boiled soured milk
Ok
They boiled soured milk, milk from goats
they boiled this soured milk over a flame , and they added to it fried chopped onion
finely chopped, which they had fried in a little ghee
after boiling the soured milk, they put it (= the fried onion) into it,
and crumbled the bread, they chop up the šrāk bread
and add it to the boiled sour milk,and they eat it. That’s what they call ‘fuwwīr’
Fuwwīr… and what else is there?
They also make rušūf
Aren’t there any sweet things at a wedding?
The sweets, the groom's mother would bring (them) if she is present.
She brings nuts and regular sweets.
Ok
So the sweets and things like these (referring to sweet-tasting items served at celebrations) are mixed and put into bags, and they are distributed during the evening gathering.
The gathering (التعليلة) lasts for seven days, and on the first day, sweets are distributed to everyone present.
The mother of...even for the men, right?
Some people distribute to the men, and some do not.
Sometimes they distribute (them) to the men, sometimes no, only to women
Only to women
For he men they prepare 'lazaqi', they make things like that
What are lazāgi? How do they make them?
Lazāgi are one of the sweet dishes which the Bedouin make.
They knead the dough, which is flour,
Just flour alone with a little salt and water; they soften it(?), they make it soft
So the dough's water is light, not like bread dough
Ok
They make it on the ṣāǧ
Then they cut it and put sugar and ghee or sugar and oil on it
Sugar and ghee or sugar and oil like that
Like bread, it should be thin, like šrāk bread, but the dough is liquid, not solid
They serve it to people, and people eat from it. They make it at funerals and at celebrations too
The lazāgi?
Yes, and there are also some sweets called bačīla.
Yes, the bačīla.
From our sweets. [for] the bačīla they bring wheat grains.
They clean it, wash it, and sift it from everything in it, and they sieve it
And they roast/heat it, heat, just enough for the wheat to cook, but they only roast it.
They grind/crack it in a grinder or take it to the mills
Before, there were steam mills where they would grind the wheat (to make) flour, and... before, there were no bakeries; they would bake at home
They made bread at home?
Yes
When did bakeries start to exist?
So bakeries started here in ar-ruǧm around the 1980s, I think,
In the 80s
Even after the 80s
So at the beginning of the 90s
Yes, aound the 90s
and before that, every one was baking at home
Before that, they bake and knead at home, either farrā​n bread or ṣāǧ bread
Ok
Rarely... rarely I mean one would go on a trip to Saḥāb (a place) to buy market bread
we call it market bread... it's rare to find people who don't bake; everyone bakes, there's no one who doesn't (NOT SURE)
All women know how to bake
All know how to bake and knead
Fine, so, this was for example the wheat season when wheat is produced
The wheat, let´s finish with the bačīla. They roast wheat over heat
on something big, on a hotplate (ṣāǧ), on something. When it is cooked, they take it off the heat and grind it
this bačīla. Then they heat some water, and they put… they knead it with sugar, with water and sugar,
and knead it until it becomes soft, and decorate it with homemade ghee and raisins which they put on the top,
and they eat it.
They eat it with spoons?
Yes, with spoons…. with their hands, there were no spoons.
With their hands?
Yes, in the old days there were no spoons…only rarely… but now it is eaten only with spoons.
That’s bačīla
It’s really delicious.
The most delicious thing (sweet)?
Yes, delicious.
So...this...
The lazāgi is delicious
The lazāgi is delicious. What else is there?
We mentioned the rušūf, they cook the rušūf
No, sweets, sweets
Sweets? These sweets, they buy the sweets (from) Čāsbān, they purchase sweets from the market, (such as) licorice and other similar items.
Ok
But these are the main types of sweets, such as lazāgi and bačīla. They also make hayṭaliyya
The hayṭaliyya
The hayṭaliyya is from milk, they milk
Yes
And they bring ǧarīša (cracked wheat), which is a type of wheat, but the ǧarīša must be
(thoroughly) washed or ground
this means it contains starch - wheat starch
they soak it in hot water and continue pressing/squeezing it until they extratc the starch from it
Then they add it to the milk, they put sugar of course with the milk. As the milk boils, they keep stirring it until it thickens, becoming firm like hayṭaliyya, similar to kāstard, like mḥalabiyya
but this hayṭaliyya is more delicious because it...
Milk
Because it contains milk, because it is made with craked wheat
Because it's made with cracked wheat, not starch... they don't add starch to it
They make it with ckacked wheat, and it tastes delicious. They extract the starch from the cracked wheat, they wring it out
make with it the hayṭaliyya
Fine, now tell about the winter food, how was the winter food, there was no crop, there was nothing (?), what do they do?
The winter food how important was, they store wheat, they store...they grow lentils, and grow...
Of course, there were no refrigerators, and there was no electricity.
There was not, they cook... they were, how did they preserve [things]? They developed their method to store with them the things/ items
How?
For example, the meat
They made the gišīm
What is this gišīm ?
This gišīm, they melt the sheep fat
The fat, as soon as/after slaughtering the sheep, they collect the fat, melt it down, and add special spices to it.
It becomes yellow, like the ghee, when the fat melts, and they store it in jars
They would put pieces of meat in it, if they want to preserve it. This was back in the day, before the 1970s. Now we have refrigerators and they preserve
Yes, this was before refrigerators
I mean that before refrigerators, they place it (the meat there), and preserve it in inside, and when they wanted to cook, they take it out from it and cook
Ok
Also very good
And the meat is preserved for example 2 months?
This is like the ghee, the local ghee, the fat, I mean they cook in it
The dihn is the one from the cattle, from..
They cook in it also the meat?
They cook in it everything
Everything they cook in it is delicious, yes, they cook lentil soup and also prepare the ḥelwiyya
What is this ḥa(e)lwiyya ?
The ḥelwiyya is also a type of wheat, ground but finely
Finely
Finely ground, and they cook it with tomatoes. They crush the tomatoes or use tomato water, tomato paste this is what they call it
They cook with it, and eat it, it is good
I mean with the wheat
With the wheat
Before they rarely cook rice, most of their food/ dishes were with wheat
Now it is the other way round, everything has rice
Now it is all with rice, now people rarely eat wheat, they cook burgul
They cook mǧi(e)ddarat of burgul, these were the winter dishes, hey also cook potatoes with,I mean the vegetables were seasonal back then unlike today [where] they're always available.
There was for example...
They use to dry tomatoes
When tomatoes were in season, they would slice them, sprinkle salt on them, [place them] on a large trays (sdūr) , and dry them in the sun
once [the tomatoes] fully dry, they would store them.
And it stays like that
They stay/remain dried, then, when they wanted to use them, they pour hot water over them, press them, and continue mashing them.
When they make it, in other days when was the liban season, mahin (?) they make ghee in summer
So how did they make ghee then?
They made ghee and marīs
Ok
They made the labaniyya, this is the one with olive oil
Labaniyya is the round yogurt?
Yes this, hey store it like provisions, so when it's no longer available,
They eat from it
Fine, how would one make ghee or labana or...?
The ghee itself, of course the milk is the basis of everything.
Milk, there must be milk
Yes, it is milk that one makes ghee from it
They make butter from it, the yogurt, the marīs, the labana
That we call gaṭīʕ not labana
It is sour
Yes it is a little sour, this, they first milk the milk
Ok
They ferment it
Ahat is this fermentation?
They make the rayeb
They add rawba, which is old rayeb yogurt theayy had/ they saved from the day before
They let it ferment, meaning they allow the fermentation process and make rayeb yogurt.
Before, they prepare saʕin and girgaʕa depending on the milk it had
How comes, what is the difference?
The girgaʕa is big
made from a ram or something big, which means it is large, it holds a significant amount of milk
Fine, this was for who had a lot of cattle
For those who have only a few sheep, it is saʕin.
The saʕin they take /make it from the skin of the she-goat, they prepare it, they skin it carefully without tearing it
They skin it, tan the hide, they make it with the tanning agent
What is this tanning agent?
The tanning agent is bought from the spice vendor/ herbalist and turns its color in red
to prevent spoilage, they first sprinkle salt on it, and prepare it the process is lengthy
they bring alum and sprinkle it on, and then they tan it with the tanning agent
then they clean it, add rayeb yogurt, and continue to churn it
They put rayeb yogurt and milk in the..?
No, they put rayeb yogurt
They fermet the rayeb
How do they ferment?
The rayeb, they bring the milk, strain it after milking, and then strain it again.
They heat it over the fire, heat it but not too high, they add a bit of rayeb yogurt that has been kept overnight, this yogurt is called rowba
They put it on it, cover it, and let it sit for 4 to 5 hours, depending on the weather. If it's warm, it does not take long
They obtain the rayeb, similar to the buttery rayeb
Ok
They obtain it, and put it in the saʕin
Fine
They keep churning it, and when they churn, what happens? the butter rises/goes up and the yogurt
this rayeb becomes butter
and yogurt
and yogurt, they take it and rassemble/collect it, of course the butter they separate (it) before
Yes
They gather it and keep it for about a month, during which no one is allowed to eat any of the butter.
They distribute the first churning, this is called the first churning. They churn and share it with all the neighbors
They put a piece butter and a bit of yogurt, and of course, we, the children, would take it and distribute it to all the neighbors
Fine
Yes, we distribute yogurt and butter to the neighbours
Had to
This is the first churning, they say to you the first churning is distributed/shared
Fine
Then the housekeeper (wife of the owner of the house), gathers the butter. For example, today she takes (some), put to churn a little butter refrigerator.
or in the one she stores in it, she churns, she churns, she churns until she gathers, she holds a large quantity stored away
Fine
They make the grāniyya
What is this grāniyya?
This grāniyya, they gather yogurt, I mean they do for the season of churning, the one who was rich for examploe, slaughters sheep,
two sheep, one sheep, depending on their means. For that who cannot, he prepare rušūf
What is the rušūf?
No, now I want to understand how we make ghee
Now, they haven't gathered the butter yet
Fine
They pour the yogurt into jars/containers
the yogurt, which is not in large quantities, they out it in white cloths, a white cloth sewed, they hang it up, allowing the water to drain
the water from it, the whey and
the yogurt itself that remains, gathers inside, what do they do with this yogurt that thickens?
What?
We do not obtain butter an yogurt
Ok
This yogurt, what, it doesn´t it remain and drain, and its whey comes out
Yes
With the remaining yogurt, they do the marīs
If they leave it loose, and if they want it to be marīš, they let it longer, I mean, for example
It sits...
Two or three weeks
Three weeks?
I mean, less, something in between, according to the weather as well
Yes
The rest... of course also according to the amout of sheep you have, I mean the one who has a lot of sheep
make, God has willed it, big cloths, that are white cloths sewed like this, the water drains from them
This water has no use
This is whey, there are people who drink it, it is good for the kidney stones, in the past, they used it to wash away dirt and dark stains.
One says to you "to whiten" (lighten the skin)
And it makes a person completely different... this whey
Yes, the one that drips/blobs
Then the one that stays/remains on the cloth solidifies the milk, what solidifies
It is called gaṭīʕ (curd), some people make it into a curd, they place it and eat it, and they add oil or a bit of butter and eat it
They dip it with a cloth, like lebana... and some people let it solidify halfway, not too much, and they make 'libaniyya,' which they roll into balls and put in oil.
This is called labaniyya
They would eat it in winter
Yes, and some people leave it, of course, and add a little salt to it
They take it out, add a little salt, and make it into marīs
mrīs
Of course the ǧamīd this is ǧamīd, what they call ǧamīd, we call it marīs
because they cure it, and they store it; once it dries, they sell it on plates, or in bags, or in something
when it completely dries... yeah... they put it in their bags... the bags that are like
white sacks, they tie them up and store them, and they put the marīs in them, of course, they add a lot of salt to it, to prevent it from spoiling... so it doesn't rot or get damaged
Now we come to the butter that we extract from the milk... yeah... it has some specific spices, they are called ḥawāyiǧ... and they use it to make what? ghee
What are these ḥawāyǧ? You mean spices?
Yeah, the spices are ḥawāyiǧ but these ḥawāyiǧ include ground spices like fenugreek, meaning each one has its own spices.
Most of the spices are the same thing
Ok
Some people add something, and some reduce the (amount of) fenugreek
According to...
The fenugreek and the ground spices, they pick them in the spring and dry them
it comes as a green herb, with yellow flowers.
a little spice... fenugreek, they roast (the fenugreek), then grind it... yeah...
then they put it on the fire
they bring a little wheat or cracked wheat, the same cracked wheat we said we squeeze to make 'libna,' the 'hayṭaliyya,' which is starch
some people do it like that, and others use flour
whoever has cracked wheat available
One does not put it
they don't use flour... cracked wheat is, of course, tastier. This is called 'gišda'
It is tasty, and then its flavor is delicious... they put butter on top of it
and this butter keeps boiling on the fire, on low heat, of course, and they skim off the foam from the top and discard it
it produces foam, and they let it continue to boil and add the spices to the butter
and the cracked wheat or flour is added, and then they stir it until the butter turns yellow
from the spices that we added.
How long does it stay on the fire?
I mean, until the butter melts.
Ok
And when it boils, it should take a light simmer like this.
then they strain it.. from the spices and the gišda
and they put / place it in jars or metal containers.
What are these gəṣṣiyāt?
These containers are the same ones we use for storing cattle cheese and the same ones we use for
Ok
But these are square jars/containers called 'samin' jars
They call them gəṣṣiyāt
Yeah, I see/recognize them, like the ones for al-ġazālīn ghee (it is brand of ghee)
No, smaller, smaller, like the ones of the goat cheese, where they put the goat/cattle cheese
Yes, yes
They put it in these buckets and close it; of course, this is used for winter
Good, what else can you tell
What else do you want (to know)? what else did they do?
The ghee, this is how we do it
I mean, as you would say, whatever you like to tell, for examples you like to tell about Bani Ṣaxar or about the al-Xaḏ̣īr
or about the region/area
By God, they are truly admirable, brave, and created with honor from God.
they love guests, and, God willing, since the time of their ancestors their homes have always been open to guests
Thanks and praise be to God.
Thank you Umm ʕaballah, thanks
